source_paper_title: Robust Sparsification for Matroid Intersection with Applications
context:
Page 6, end of Introduction (paragraph beginning “Although the above procedure can be costly in running time…”)

"Moreover, in our algorithms, as we frequently update the ground set on which we compute the decomposition by adding or removing one element, there may be room to improve our time complexity: we leave as an open question whether updating a density-based decomposition when performing these kinds of operations can be done more efficiently, without re-computing the whole decomposition each time."
title: Efficiently update the density-based decomposition under single-element insertions/deletions
latex:
Given a matroid \(M=(V,\mathcal I)\) accessed via an independence (or rank) oracle, and a maintained subset \(V'\subseteq V\). Let \(M|_{V'}\) denote the restriction of \(M\) to \(V'\). Define the density of a nonempty set \(U\subseteq V'\) as \(\rho(U)=\frac{|U|}{\mathrm{rank}_{M|_{V'}}(U)}\) (with \(\rho(\emptyset)=0\)). Define the density-based decomposition of \(V'\) to be the sequence \(U_1,\dots,U_k\) constructed greedily as follows, where \(k=\mathrm{rank}_M(V)\):
- Let \(M_1 := M|_{V'}\). Choose \(U_1\subseteq V'\) maximizing \(\rho_{M_1}(U)\), breaking ties by maximum \(|U|\).
- For \(j\ge 2\), let \(M_j := (M|_{V'})/(U_1\cup\cdots\cup U_{j-1})\). Choose \(U_j\subseteq V'\setminus (U_1\cup\cdots\cup U_{j-1})\) maximizing \(\rho_{M_j}(U)\), breaking ties by maximum \(|U|\).
(Allowing some trailing \(U_j=\emptyset\).)

Problem: Design an algorithm/data structure that maintains the density-based decomposition \(U_1,\dots,U_k\) (or equivalently the associated element densities induced by it) under updates to \(V'\) consisting of inserting or deleting a single element \(e\in V\), i.e., replacing \(V'\leftarrow V'\cup\{e\}\) or \(V'\leftarrow V'\setminus\{e\}\). The goal is to support each update in time asymptotically faster than recomputing the entire decomposition from scratch (which can be done in polynomial time via repeated densest-set computations/submodular minimization), while using polynomial space and preserving correctness of the maintained decomposition.
location_in_paper: Page 6, end of Introduction (paragraph beginning “Although the above procedure can be costly in running time…”)
open_problem_quote: "Moreover, in our algorithms, as we frequently update the ground set on which we compute the decomposition by adding or removing one element, there may be room to improve our time complexity: we leave as an open question whether updating a density-based decomposition when performing these kinds of operations can be done more efficiently, without re-computing the whole decomposition each time."
